questions.text: In Step 1 of the ER-to-Relational mapping algorithm, how are composite attributes of a regular entity type handled?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They are mapped to a separate relation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Only the simple component attributes that form the composite attribute are included in the relation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The composite attribute is kept as a single string field.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They are ignored and only simple attributes are mapped.

questions.explanation: According to Step 1, for a regular entity type, if a key is composite, the set of simple attributes that form it will together form the primary key of the relation.

questions.text: What is the primary key of a relation created from a weak entity type in Step 2?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The partial key of the weak entity type alone.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A new surrogate key generated by the DBMS.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The combination of the primary key of the owner entity and the partial key of the weak entity.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The primary key of the owner entity alone.

questions.explanation: The primary key of a weak entity relation is the combination of the primary key(s) of the owner(s) and the partial key of the weak entity type.

questions.text:
Consider the following ER fragment. Which rule applies when mapping this to a relational schema?

```mermaid
graph LR
E1[Employee] --1:1--- R((Manages))
R --- E2[Department]
style E2 stroke-width:4px
```
Note: Department has total participation in 'Manages'.

choices.label: A
choices.text: Include the PK of Employee as a foreign key in Department.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Include the PK of Department as a foreign key in Employee.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Create a new relation for 'Manages' with two foreign keys.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Merge Employee and Department into a single relation.

questions.explanation: In Step 3 (1:1 mapping), it is better to choose the entity type with total participation to house the foreign key. In this case, Department has total participation.

questions.text: When mapping a binary 1:N relationship type (Step 4), where is the foreign key placed?

choices.label: C
choices.text: In a new relation specifically for the relationship.

choices.label: D
choices.text: On both entity relations to ensure redundancy.

questions.explanation: For 1:N relationships, the primary key of the 1-side is included as a foreign key in the relation representing the N-side.

questions.text: In Step 5, which represents the mapping of binary M:N relationship types, how is the primary key of the resulting relation determined?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is a single surrogate key.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is the primary key of the entity with more attributes.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is the combination of the foreign keys referencing the participating entity types.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is whichever foreign key is designated as 'Primary' by the user.

questions.explanation: For M:N relationships, a new relation is created, and its primary key is the combination of the primary keys of the participating entity types.

questions.text: How is a multivalued attribute mapped to the relational model (Step 6)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is included as a comma-separated list in the original relation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A new relation is created including the attribute and the primary key of the parent entity as a foreign key.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Multiple columns (Attr1, Attr2, Attr3) are added to the original relation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is ignored in the relational model.

questions.explanation: Step 6 states that for each multivalued attribute, a new relation is created including the attribute and the primary key of the associated entity type.

questions.text: In mapping an n-ary relationship type where n > 2 (Step 7), what forms the primary key of the new relation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The primary key of any one of the participating entities.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A combination of the primary keys of all participating entity types.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The primary key of the relationship attribute.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The primary keys of at least two participating entities.

questions.explanation: For n-ary relationships, a new relation is created including the primary keys of all participating entity types as foreign keys, and their combination forms the primary key.

questions.text: According to the summary table, what is the relational model equivalent of an ER 'Value Set'?
questions.explanation: Table 7.1 shows that 'Value set' in the ER Model corresponds to 'Domain' in the Relational Model.

questions.text: Which mapping option for specialization/generalization involves creating a single relation with one type attribute (discriminator)?
questions.explanation: Option 8C is described as a 'Single relation with one type attribute' to indicate the subclass.

questions.text: When is Option 8B (Multiple relations—Subclass relations only) applicable for mapping specialization?

choices.label: A
choices.text: When the specialization is partial and disjoint.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Only when the specialization is total.

choices.label: C
choices.text: When there are no attributes in the subclasses.

questions.explanation: Option 8B only works for a specialization whose subclasses are total, meaning every entity in the superclass must belong to at least one subclass.

questions.text: What is the primary purpose of a 'surrogate key' in Step 9 (Mapping of Union Types)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To replace a composite primary key in a weak entity.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To provide a common key when defining superclasses have different keys.

questions.explanation: Step 9 specifies that for mapping a category where superclasses have different keys, a new surrogate key is created to correspond to the category.

questions.text: Which EER mapping option is most suitable for overlapping subclasses where a single entity can belong to multiple subclasses simultaneously?
questions.explanation: Option 8D uses multiple boolean type attributes (t1, t2, ..., tm), which allows a tuple to belong to multiple subclasses.

questions.text: In a 1:N relationship like WORKS_FOR (where 1 Department has N Employees), if we map this using Step 4, which attribute becomes the foreign key in the EMPLOYEE relation?

choices.label: C
choices.text: A new surrogate key for the relationship.

questions.explanation: Step 4 says include as foreign key in the N-side (Employee) the primary key of the 1-side (Department).

questions.text: True or False: Option 8A (Multiple relations—Superclass and subclasses) can only be used for disjoint specializations.
questions.explanation: The text states Option 8A works for any specialization (total or partial, disjoint or overlapping).

questions.text: If a shared subclass (Multiple Inheritance) is modeled as a category, it is because:

choices.label: B
choices.text: The superclasses have different key attributes.

choices.label: D
choices.text: There are no attributes in the subclasses.

questions.explanation: Step 9 and the section on shared subclasses state that if classes do not have the same key attribute, the shared subclass must be modeled as a category (Union Type).

questions.text: What does a 'Relationship relation' in the Relational Model represent in the ER Model for an M:N relationship?
questions.explanation: According to Table 7.1, an M:N relationship type is represented by a 'Relationship' relation and two foreign keys.

questions.text: When mapping a 1:1 relationship using the Foreign Key approach (Step 3), why is total participation important?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It prevents the foreign key from having NULL values.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It ensures the primary key is composite.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It eliminates the need for a foreign key.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is only a suggestion and has no technical impact.

questions.explanation: Choosing the entity with total participation for the foreign key minimizes null values because every entity in that relation must participate in the relationship.

questions.text: In mapping a multivalued attribute `Locations` for a `Department` entity (PK: `Dnumber`), the relation `DEPT_LOCATIONS` is created. What is the primary key of this new relation?
questions.explanation: Step 6 specifies that the primary key of the new relation R is the combination of the multivalued attribute A and the foreign key K (owner's PK).

questions.text: In Step 8, Option 8D, what type of attributes are \( t_1, t_2, \dots, t_m \)?
questions.explanation: Option 8D uses Boolean type attributes to indicate whether a tuple belongs to a specific subclass.

questions.text: How are simple attributes of an n-ary relationship type handled in Step 7?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They are included as attributes in the new relationship relation S.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are added to one of the participating entity relations.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They must be moved to a weak entity.

questions.explanation: Step 7 states: 'Also include any simple attributes of the n-ary relationship type... as attributes of S.'

questions.text: Given the relationship `SUPPLY` between `Supplier`, `Part`, and `Project`, which mapping step would be applied?
questions.explanation: A relationship between three entities (Supplier, Part, Project) is a ternary relationship, which falls under Step 7 (N-ary).

questions.text: In Option 8A of mapping specialization, if the superclass is `Person` and the subclasses are `Employee` and `Alumnus`, how many relations are created?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Depends on the number of attributes.

questions.explanation: Option 8A creates one relation for the superclass (Person) and one relation for each subclass (Employee, Alumnus), totaling 3.

questions.text: In Option 8B, what happens to the attributes of the superclass?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They are stored in a separate superclass relation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are included in each of the subclass relations.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Only the primary key of the superclass is kept in subclass relations.

questions.explanation: In Option 8B, each subclass relation contains the attributes of that subclass plus all attributes of the superclass.

questions.text: Consider an ER diagram where a `Car` is an entity and `Engine_Size` is a simple attribute. Which step is used to map this?
questions.explanation: Step 1 covers the mapping of regular entity types and their simple attributes.

questions.text: What is the primary key of the `WORKS_ON` relation in the COMPANY example (M:N between Employee and Project)?
questions.explanation: In Step 5 for M:N relationships, the primary key is the combination of the foreign keys (ESSN and PNO in this case).

questions.text: Identify the error in this statement: 'In Step 2, a weak entity's primary key is identical to its owner's primary key.'

choices.label: A
choices.text: A weak entity does not have a primary key.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The primary key must also include the partial key of the weak entity.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The primary key must be a surrogate key only.

questions.explanation: Step 2 states the primary key is the combination of the owner's PK and the weak entity's partial key.

questions.text: In a specialization where a `Vehicle` can be a `Car` OR a `Truck` (disjoint), which single-relation option is most efficient?
questions.explanation: Option 8C uses a single relation with one type attribute, which is ideal for disjoint subclasses.

questions.text:
```mermaid
erDiagram
    OWNER ||--o{ PROPERTY : owns
    OWNER {
        string OwnerID PK
    }
    PERSON ||--|| OWNER : is-a
    BANK ||--|| OWNER : is-a
    COMPANY ||--|| OWNER : is-a
```
Which mapping step handles the 'OWNER' structure above?
questions.explanation: This is a Category (Union Type) where multiple entity types (Person, Bank, Company) act as superclasses for a single subclass (Owner). This is mapped using Step 9.

questions.text: What is meant by 'surrogate key' in the context of Category mapping?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A key derived from the owner entity.

choices.label: B
choices.text: An artificial attribute used as a primary key when no natural common key exists.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A foreign key that points to multiple tables.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A key consisting of all attributes in the relation.

questions.explanation: In Step 9, a surrogate key is created when superclasses of a category have different keys to provide a uniform PK for the category relation.

questions.text: In Step 4, if the 1:N relationship has its own attribute (e.g., `StartDate`), where is this attribute placed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: In the relation of the 1-side entity.

choices.label: B
choices.text: In the relation of the N-side entity.

choices.label: C
choices.text: In a separate relation for the relationship.

questions.explanation: Step 4 states: 'Include any simple attributes of the 1:N relation type as attributes of S (the N-side relation).'

questions.text: The relationship `SUPERVISION` is a recursive 1:N relationship where an employee supervises other employees. How is this mapped?

choices.label: A
choices.text: By adding a foreign key (e.g., `Super_SSN`) to the Employee relation referencing its own Primary Key.

choices.label: C
choices.text: By adding a multivalued attribute to the Employee relation.

questions.explanation: A recursive 1:N relationship is handled by Step 4: the PK of the 1-side (supervisor) is added as a foreign key to the N-side (supervisee). Since both are the same relation, it's a self-referencing FK.

questions.text:
Match the following (ER to Relational):
1. Entity Type
2. M:N Relationship
3. Multivalued Attribute

Options:
X. Relation + Foreign Key
Y. Relationship Relation + 2 Foreign Keys
Z. Entity Relation
questions.explanation: Based on Table 7.1: Entity Type -> Entity relation; M:N relationship -> Relationship relation and 2 FKs; Multivalued attribute -> Relation and FK.

questions.text: Which mapping option for EER models creates a relation for every subclass but *no* relation for the superclass?
questions.explanation: Option 8B is 'Multiple relations—Subclass relations only'.

questions.text: What is the primary constraint of Option 8C when dealing with subclasses?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It can only handle total specializations.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It can result in many NULL values if the subclasses have many specific attributes.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It requires surrogate keys for all subclasses.

questions.explanation: Since Option 8C puts everything in one relation, attributes belonging to other subclasses will be NULL for a given tuple.

questions.text: In a specialization lattice (Multiple Inheritance), a subclass inherits attributes from:

choices.label: C
choices.text: Only the superclass with the same name.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The database schema, not specific classes.

questions.explanation: Multiple inheritance means a subclass (like STUDENT_ASSISTANT) is a subclass of several classes and inherits from all of them.

questions.text: In Step 3, if both sides of a 1:1 relationship have partial participation, which approach is often used if no side is total?
questions.explanation: While Step 3 highlights the Foreign Key approach, the general relational mapping principles (and the summary table) suggest that 1:1 can also use a relationship relation, especially to avoid NULLs in partial participation.

questions.text: What relational concept corresponds to an ER 'Key Attribute'?
questions.explanation: Table 7.1 shows that 'Key attribute' corresponds to 'Primary (or secondary) key'.

questions.text: For a weak entity with multiple levels of owners (e.g., Room belongs to Building, Building belongs to Campus), its Primary Key will include:

choices.label: B
choices.text: The primary keys of all its ancestors plus its partial key.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Only the primary key of the immediate owner.

questions.explanation: Step 2 is applied recursively; the weak entity's PK is the combination of its partial key and the PK of its owner (which may itself be a combination).

questions.text: When mapping a category (Union Type) in Step 9, if all superclasses share the same key attribute, is a surrogate key mandatory?

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, the shared key can be used as the primary key.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, but only if there are attributes on the category itself.

questions.explanation: Step 9 specifies surrogate keys are used 'customary' when superclasses have *different* keys. If they share a key, that key can be used.

questions.text:
```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Entity] ---|Multivalued| B(Attribute)
```
If 'Attribute' B is composite with components B1 and B2, the relation for B will contain:

choices.label: B
choices.text: Two columns: B1 and B2, plus the owner PK.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A separate relation for B1 and another for B2.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nothing, composite multivalued attributes are not allowed.

questions.explanation: Step 6 states: 'If the multivalued attribute is composite, we include its simple components.'

questions.text: In Step 1 of the ER-to-Relational mapping, what is created for each regular (strong) entity type E?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A foreign key in a related relation

choices.label: B
choices.text: A relation R that includes all simple attributes of E

choices.label: C
choices.text: A new relation for each attribute of E

choices.label: D
choices.text: A surrogate key for the entity

questions.explanation: According to Step 1, for each regular entity type, a relation R is created containing all simple attributes of that entity.

questions.text: If a regular entity type has a composite key, how is the primary key of the resulting relation formed in Step 1?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A single new attribute is created to replace it

choices.label: B
choices.text: Only the first simple attribute of the composite key is used

choices.label: C
choices.text: The set of simple attributes that form the composite key together form the primary key

choices.label: D
choices.text: Composite keys are not allowed in relational mapping

questions.explanation: Step 1 specifies that if the chosen key is composite, the set of simple attributes forming it will together form the primary key of the relation.

questions.text: Which of the following is true regarding the mapping of weak entity types (Step 2)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They do not require their own relation

choices.label: B
choices.text: The relation includes the primary key of the owner entity as a foreign key

choices.label: C
choices.text: The primary key is the same as the owner entity's primary key

choices.label: D
choices.text: Weak entities only contain their partial keys

questions.explanation: Step 2 states that for a weak entity, the relation must include the primary key of the owner entity type as a foreign key.

questions.text: In the mapping of the weak entity type **DEPENDENT**, what is the primary key of the resulting relation?
questions.explanation: The primary key is the combination of the owner's primary key (ESSN) and the weak entity's partial key (DEPENDENT_NAME).

questions.text: In Step 3, when mapping a binary 1:1 relationship type using the Foreign Key approach, which relation is it better to choose to include the foreign key?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Either relation, it makes no difference

choices.label: D
choices.text: The relation with the most attributes

questions.explanation: Step 3 suggests it is better to choose an entity type with total participation to serve in the role of the relation containing the foreign key.

questions.text: According to the example in Step 3, why is the **DEPARTMENT** relation chosen to include the foreign key for the **MANAGES** relationship?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Because every employee manages a department

choices.label: B
choices.text: Because its participation in the relationship is total

choices.label: C
choices.text: Because it has fewer attributes than EMPLOYEE

choices.label: D
choices.text: Because it is a strong entity

questions.explanation: The text states DEPARTMENT is chosen because its participation in the MANAGES relationship type is total.

questions.text: When mapping a binary 1:N relationship (Step 4), where should the foreign key be placed?

choices.label: B
choices.text: In the relation representing the N-side

questions.explanation: Step 4 specifies including the primary key of the 1-side relation as a foreign key in the N-side relation (S).

questions.text: In the example of the **WORKS_FOR** relationship, which attribute is added to the **EMPLOYEE** relation?
questions.explanation: For WORKS_FOR (a 1:N relationship), the primary key DNUMBER of DEPARTMENT is included as foreign key DNO in the EMPLOYEE relation.

questions.text: How are simple attributes of a 1:N relationship type handled in Step 4?

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are included as attributes of the relation on the 1-side

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are included as attributes of the relation on the N-side

choices.label: D
choices.text: A new relation is created for them

questions.explanation: Step 4 states to include any simple attributes of the 1:N relation type as attributes of S (the N-side relation).

questions.text: Which step requires the creation of a new relation S to represent the relationship itself?
questions.explanation: Step 5 for M:N relationships requires creating a new relation S to represent the relationship.

questions.text: In the mapping of the **WORKS_ON** M:N relationship, what is the primary key of the resulting relation?
questions.explanation: The primary key of the WORKS_ON relation is the combination of the foreign keys referencing the participating entities (ESSN and PNO).

questions.text: What is the result of mapping a multivalued attribute A according to Step 6?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A new column is added to the original relation

choices.label: B
choices.text: A new relation R is created including A and the primary key of the parent entity

choices.label: C
choices.text: The attribute is ignored in the relational model

choices.label: D
choices.text: A comma-separated list is stored in a single field

questions.explanation: Step 6 states to create a new relation R that includes the attribute A plus the primary key of the parent entity as a foreign key.

questions.text: In mapping the **LOCATIONS** multivalued attribute of **DEPARTMENT**, what is the primary key of the **DEPT_LOCATIONS** relation?

choices.label: D
choices.text: A combination of DNAME and DLOCATION

questions.explanation: The primary key of the relation created for a multivalued attribute is the combination of the attribute itself and the foreign key referencing the parent entity.

questions.text: For an n-ary relationship type where n > 2 (Step 7), how is the primary key of the new relation S determined?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is the primary key of the first participating entity

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is a combination of all primary keys of the participating entity types

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is a new surrogate key

choices.label: D
choices.text: It depends on the participation constraints

questions.explanation: Step 7 states that the primary key of the relation representing an n-ary relationship is usually the combination of the foreign keys of the participating entity types.

questions.text: In the mapping of the ternary relationship **SUPPLY**, which attributes form the primary key?
questions.explanation: According to the Step 7 example, the primary key of the relation SUPPLY is the combination of the three foreign keys {SNAME, PARTNO, PROJNAME}.

questions.text: Based on Table 7.1, what does a 'Multivalued attribute' in the ER model correspond to in the Relational Model?
questions.explanation: Table 7.1 lists 'Relation and foreign key' as the relational correspondence for a Multivalued attribute.

questions.text: Based on Table 7.1, what is the relational equivalent of an 'Entity type'?
questions.explanation: Table 7.1 defines the correspondence of an Entity type as an “Entity” relation.

questions.text: How is a composite attribute handled during ER-to-Relational mapping according to Table 7.1?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is mapped as a single string attribute

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is mapped as a set of simple component attributes

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is mapped to a separate relation

questions.explanation: Table 7.1 states that a Composite attribute corresponds to a 'Set of simple component attributes'.

questions.text: Which option for mapping specialization/generalization involves creating a relation for the superclass and separate relations for each subclass?
questions.explanation: Option 8A is described as 'Multiple relations—Superclass and subclasses'.

questions.text: In Option 8A, what is the primary key of the subclass relations?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A new unique ID for each subclass

choices.label: B
choices.text: The same primary key as the superclass relation

choices.label: D
choices.text: A combination of the subclass name and the superclass key

questions.explanation: In Option 8A, PK(Li) = k, where k is the primary key of the superclass relation L.

questions.text: For which type of specialization is Option 8B (Subclass relations only) strictly appropriate?
questions.explanation: Option 8B description states: 'This option only works for a specialization whose subclasses are total'.

questions.text: In Option 8C, what is the purpose of the attribute 't'?

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is a boolean flag for total participation

questions.explanation: Option 8C uses attribute 't' as a type or discriminating attribute to indicate which subclass each tuple belongs to.

questions.text: Which mapping option is most suitable for overlapping subclasses in a single relation?
questions.explanation: Option 8D (Single relation with multiple type attributes) uses boolean attributes \( t_i \) to indicate membership in potentially multiple (overlapping) subclasses.

questions.text: What is a 'surrogate key' in the context of mapping Union Types (Categories)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A key inherited from the first superclass

choices.label: B
choices.text: A new key attribute specified for the category relation

choices.label: C
choices.text: A foreign key pointing to all superclasses

choices.label: D
choices.text: A key that must be a composite of all superclass keys

questions.explanation: Step 9 explains that for union types with different keys, it is customary to specify a new key attribute called a surrogate key.

questions.text: In the example of the **OWNER** category, what is the surrogate key called?
questions.explanation: Step 35 mentions creating a relation OWNER with a surrogate key called OwnerId.

questions.text: What is multiple inheritance in the context of EER mapping?

choices.label: B
choices.text: A subclass belonging to more than one superclass

choices.label: D
choices.text: An entity having multiple primary keys

questions.explanation: A shared subclass (like STUDENT_ASSISTANT) is a subclass of several classes, indicating multiple inheritance.

questions.text: In shared subclasses (multiple inheritance), what is the requirement regarding key attributes of the superclasses?

choices.label: B
choices.text: They must all have the same key attribute

choices.label: C
choices.text: They must all be composite keys

choices.label: D
choices.text: There are no restrictions on keys

questions.explanation: The text states: 'These classes must all have the same key attribute; otherwise, the shared subclass would be modeled as a category'.

questions.text: Which options can be applied to map a shared subclass (multiple inheritance)?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Any of the options discussed in Step 8 (subject to restrictions)

questions.explanation: Step 37 states: 'We can apply any of the options discussed in Step 8 to a shared subclass'.

questions.text: In the relational schema result for the **COMPANY** ER diagram, what relation represents the **LOCATIONS** multivalued attribute of **DEPARTMENT**?
questions.explanation: Example 18 mentions the creation of the relation DEPT_LOCATIONS for this purpose.

questions.text: What constitutes the primary key of a relation S created for a binary M:N relationship R?

choices.label: B
choices.text: The combination of foreign keys referencing the participating entity types

choices.label: C
choices.text: The primary key of the 'Many' side

choices.label: D
choices.text: The simple attributes of the relationship

questions.explanation: Step 5 states: 'Include as foreign key attributes in S the primary keys of the relations that represent the participating entity types; their combination will form the primary key of S'.

questions.text: Under Option 8C, if an entity belongs to subclass \( S_2 \) but not \( S_1 \), what happens to the attributes of \( S_1 \) in that tuple?

choices.label: C
choices.text: The tuple is split into two relations

choices.label: D
choices.text: This situation is not possible in 8C

questions.explanation: While not explicitly stated in the summary text, Option 8C creates a single relation with all subclass attributes; logically, attributes of subclasses to which the entity does not belong would be NULL.

questions.text: Which step in the mapping algorithm handles Union Types (Categories)?
questions.explanation: Step 9 is dedicated to the mapping of Union Types (Categories).

questions.text: In Step 5, what attribute of the **WORKS_ON** relation represents the hours worked?
questions.explanation: Step 16 states: 'Attribute HOURS in WORKS_ON represents the HOURS attribute of the relation type'.

questions.text: Which of the following mapping options for specialization works for ANY specialization (total or partial, disjoint or overlapping)?
questions.explanation: Step 26 states that Option 8A 'works for any specialization (total or partial, disjoint of overlapping)'.

questions.text: What is the primary key of the **EMPLOYEE** relation in the mapping example provided?
questions.explanation: Step 6 mentions that 'SSN, DNUMBER, and PNUMBER are the primary keys for the relations EMPLOYEE, DEPARTMENT, and PROJECT'.

questions.text: How many foreign keys would a relation created for a ternary (3-ary) relationship type typically have?
questions.explanation: According to Step 7 and Table 7.1, an n-ary relationship maps to a relation with n foreign keys.

questions.text: In mapping a 1:1 relationship, if one side has partial participation and the other has total participation, which approach is recommended?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Place the Foreign Key in the relation with total participation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Place the Foreign Key in the relation with partial participation

questions.explanation: Step 10 states: 'It is better to choose an entity type with total participation in R in the role of S' (the relation containing the foreign key).

questions.text: True or False: In Option 8D, each subclass is represented by a Boolean attribute.
questions.explanation: Step 32 states: 'Each \( t_i \) ... is a Boolean type attribute indicating whether a tuple belongs to the subclass \( S_i \)'.

questions.text: In the relational model correspondence for a key attribute (Table 7.1), what is it mapped to?
questions.explanation: Table 7.1 lists 'Primary (or secondary) key' as the correspondence for a 'Key attribute'.

questions.text: When mapping a Union Type (Category) where superclasses have different keys, what is Step 9's custom recommendation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Use the key of the first superclass only

choices.label: B
choices.text: Create a composite key from all superclasses

choices.label: C
choices.text: Specify a new surrogate key attribute

choices.label: D
choices.text: Convert the category back into a standard entity

questions.explanation: Step 33 states: 'it is customary to specify a new key attribute, called a surrogate key, when creating a relation to correspond to the category'.
